situations.title: Fruit, Farms & the Fazenda
situations.summary: Talking produce like a Madeiran: banana da Madeira, anona, maracujá, semilhas, what is in season, whose fazenda it comes from, and swapping garden surplus with neighbours.

phrase_patterns.translation: Do you have {produce} from your farm?

variants.translation: Do you (formal) have {produce} from your farm?
variants.note: Use 'Tem' for formal address ('o senhor/a senhora').

phrase_patterns.translation: Do you want to swap {item1} for {item2}?

phrase_patterns.base: A minha fazenda está a dar {fruta} este ano.
phrase_patterns.translation: My farm is producing {fruit} this year.

vocabulary.note: In Madeira, often refers to a small agricultural plot or garden.
Madeiran regionalism for 'batata'.
A popular fruit in Madeira, especially in season.
Commonly grown in Madeira, used in poncha and desserts.
Often used when talking about extra produce from a garden or farm.
Refers to the time of year when a fruit or vegetable is in season.

dialogues.title: Neighbours and the Harvest
dialogues.context: Maria and João, neighbours with small fazendas, are chatting after a good harvest.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Bom dia, João! Que boas anonas tens aí! A tua fazenda está a dar bem este ano, não está?
lines.translation: Good morning, João! What good custard apples you have there! Your farm is producing well this year, isn't it?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Bom dia, Maria! Pois é, a colheita foi ótima. Queres levar umas? Tenho excedente.
lines.translation: Good morning, Maria! Indeed, the harvest was great. Do you want to take some? I have a surplus.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Ah, sim, obrigada! As minhas semilhas também estão a crescer imenso. Queres um saco?
lines.translation: Oh, yes, thank you! My potatoes are also growing a lot. Do you want a bag?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: P'ra já! Assim trocamos. A minha fazenda não tem semilhas este ano. Está na época do maracujá também, já viste?
lines.translation: Right away! This way we swap. My farm doesn't have potatoes this year. It's passion fruit season too, have you seen?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Sim, já apanhei alguns. Estão muito doces. É bom ter a nossa fruta fresca, não é?
lines.translation: Yes, I've already picked some. They are very sweet. It's good to have our fresh fruit, isn't it?

cultural_notes.title: The 'Fazenda' in Madeira
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, a 'fazenda' often refers to a small plot of land or garden, rather than a large commercial farm. Many Madeirans cultivate their own fruits, vegetables, and herbs, even in urban areas. It's common for neighbours to share or swap their surplus produce, fostering a strong sense of community and self-sufficiency.

cultural_notes.title: Madeiran Produce and Seasonality
cultural_notes.body: Madeira's climate allows for a wide variety of tropical and subtropical fruits. Key produce includes 'banana da Madeira' (smaller and sweeter than typical bananas), 'anona' (custard apple), 'maracujá' (passion fruit, in many varieties), and 'semilhas' (potatoes, a local term). Knowing what's in season ('está na época') is a common topic of conversation and helps when choosing fresh produce.

roleplay.scenario: You're walking past your neighbour, D. Fátima, who is tending her small garden (fazenda). She offers you some fresh produce.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Tudo bem? Tenho aqui umas bananas da Madeira, fresquinhas da minha fazenda. Queres levar um cacho?
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! All good? I have some Madeiran bananas here, fresh from my farm. Do you want to take a bunch?

options.text: Ah, sim, muito obrigada! São as melhores!
options.translation: Oh, yes, thank you very much! They are the best!
options.feedback: Great start! You accepted politely and complimented the produce.

options.text: Que bom! E anonas, não tens este ano?
options.translation: How nice! And custard apples, don't you have any this year?
options.feedback: Good, you're asking about other produce. This shows interest in her fazenda.

options.text: Sim, obrigada! Eu tenho umas semilhas em casa, queres trocar?
options.translation: Yes, thank you! I have some potatoes at home, do you want to swap?
options.feedback: Excellent! You're engaging in the common Madeiran practice of swapping produce.

nodes.npc_text: De nada! Leva, leva. E o teu jardim, está a dar alguma coisa boa?
nodes.npc_translation: You're welcome! Take them, take them. And your garden, is it producing anything good?

options.text: Sim, tenho umas alfaces e uns tomates. Mas não tenho uma fazenda como a sua!
options.translation: Yes, I have some lettuces and some tomatoes. But I don't have a farm like yours!
options.feedback: Good, you mentioned your own produce and complimented her fazenda.

options.text: Não muito, este ano. O tempo não ajudou.
options.translation: Not much, this year. The weather didn't help.
options.feedback: A realistic response. You can share a small complaint about the weather.

nodes.npc_text: Anonas? Ah, as anonas ainda não estão na época, sabes. Só mais p'ra o outono. Mas tenho uns maracujás roxos, se quiseres.
nodes.npc_translation: Custard apples? Oh, the custard apples aren't in season yet, you know. Only more towards autumn. But I have some purple passion fruits, if you want.

options.text: Ah, pois é! Esqueci-me. Maracujás roxos, que bom! Levo sim.
options.translation: Oh, that's right! I forgot. Purple passion fruits, how nice! I'll take them, yes.
options.feedback: Good, you acknowledged the seasonality and accepted the offer.

options.text: Não, obrigada. Só gosto das amarelas.
options.translation: No, thank you. I only like the yellow ones.
options.feedback: A bit direct, but valid. You can decline politely.

nodes.npc_text: Semilhas? Que maravilha! Eu adoro as tuas semilhas. Sim, trocamos! Leva as bananas e eu passo depois p'ra apanhar as semilhas.
nodes.npc_translation: Potatoes? How wonderful! I love your potatoes. Yes, let's swap! Take the bananas and I'll come by later to pick up the potatoes.

options.text: Combinado! Fico à espera. Bom trabalho na sua fazenda!
options.translation: It's a deal! I'll be waiting. Good work on your farm!
options.feedback: Excellent! You confirmed the swap and ended with a polite compliment.

nodes.npc_text: Até já! Depois falamos.
nodes.npc_translation: See you soon! We'll talk later.

mission.title: Local Produce Talk
prep: Review the names of common Madeiran fruits and vegetables: anona, maracujá, banana da Madeira, semilha.
Practice asking about what's in season: 'Está na época da anona agora?'
Prepare to offer to swap produce or ask if someone has produce from their 'fazenda'.
likely_responses: Que bom! As minhas também estão a dar bem.

goals: Identify and name at least three Madeiran fruits or vegetables.
Ask if a specific fruit or vegetable is in season.
Offer to swap garden produce with a neighbour.
Understand and use the term 'fazenda' in a Madeiran context.

review_items.prompt: Say in Portuguese: It's {fruit} season now.
Say in Portuguese: Do you have {produce} from your farm?
Say in Portuguese: Do you want to swap {item1} for {item2}?
Say in Portuguese: My farm is producing {fruit} this year.
review_items.answer: A minha fazenda está a dar {fruta} este ano.

review_items.prompt: Recall the Portuguese for: small farm / garden plot
Recall the Portuguese for: potato
Recall the Portuguese for: custard apple
Recall the Portuguese for: passion fruit
Listen to the dialogue at natural speed and answer: what is being asked for?

situations.title: Football & Sport Small Talk
situations.summary: The safest conversation on the island: Marítimo and Nacional, last night’s match, the league table — reacting, lamenting and celebrating without needing perfect grammar.

vocabulary.note: Common term in sports for rankings.
Used for sports fans.
Often a topic of discussion after a match.
Sports club, e.g., 'o meu clube' (my team).

dialogues.title: After the Match
dialogues.context: At a local café, two neighbours, João and Maria, discuss the previous night's football match.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Bom dia, Maria! Viste o jogo do Marítimo ontem?
lines.translation: Good morning, Maria! Did you see the Marítimo game yesterday?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Bom dia, João! Vi, pois. Que pena, não é? Não jogaram nada de jeito!
lines.translation: Good morning, João! Yes, I did. What a pity, isn't it? They didn't play well at all!

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Pois é! E a arbitragem? Um desastre! Acho que o resultado não foi justo.
lines.translation: Exactly! And the refereeing? A disaster! I don't think the result was fair.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Concordo. Assim, a classificação fica mais complicada. Mas pronto, é o futebol.
lines.translation: I agree. This way, the league table gets more complicated. But oh well, that's football.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: É verdade. Mas o Nacional está a jogar bem este ano, pelo menos. Há sempre esperança!
lines.translation: It's true. But Nacional is playing well this year, at least. There's always hope!

dialogues.title: Celebrating a Win
dialogues.context: At the market, a vendor and a customer, Ana and Pedro, are chatting about a recent win.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Então, Pedro, viste a vitória do Nacional no fim de semana?
lines.translation: So, Pedro, did you see Nacional's victory this weekend?

lines.speaker: Pedro
lines.text: Claro que sim, Ana! Que jogaço! Gostei imenso de ver. Jogaram mesmo bem!
lines.translation: Of course, Ana! What a great game! I really enjoyed watching it. They played really well!

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Pois é! A classificação está muito melhor agora. Acho que podemos sonhar com a subida.
lines.translation: Exactly! The league table is much better now. I think we can dream of promotion.

lines.speaker: Pedro
lines.text: Oxalá! Os adeptos merecem uma boa época. Espero que continuem assim.
lines.translation: Hopefully! The fans deserve a good season. I hope they continue like this.

cultural_notes.title: Football in Madeira: Marítimo vs. Nacional
cultural_notes.body: Football is a central part of Madeiran culture, and conversations about the local teams, Marítimo and Nacional, are extremely common. These two clubs have a strong rivalry, and people often identify strongly with one or the other. Discussing matches, results, and the league table is an easy way to engage in small talk with locals. It's common to express strong opinions, both positive and negative, about team performance and referee decisions.

cultural_notes.title: Expressing Opinions and Agreement
cultural_notes.body: When discussing football, it's natural to express agreement or disagreement. Phrases like 'Pois é!' (Exactly!), 'Concordo' (I agree), or 'É verdade' (It's true) are common for agreement. For opinions, 'Acho que...' (I think that...) is very useful. Madeirans often use interjections and exclamations to convey emotion, such as 'Que pena!' (What a pity!) or 'Que jogaço!' (What a great game!).

roleplay.scenario: You're at the local 'padaria' (bakery) buying bread. The owner, a friendly local, starts talking about the Marítimo match from last night.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Então, viste o jogo do Marítimo ontem? Que desilusão, não foi?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! So, did you see the Marítimo game yesterday? What a disappointment, wasn't it?

options.text: Bom dia! Vi, sim. Que pena, não jogaram nada bem.
options.translation: Good morning! Yes, I did. What a pity, they didn't play well at all.
options.feedback: Great start! You agreed and expressed disappointment naturally.

options.text: Olá! Não vi o jogo. Como é que foi?
options.translation: Hello! I didn't see the game. How was it?
options.feedback: Good, it's okay not to have seen it. Asking 'How was it?' is a good follow-up.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é, uma vergonha! Assim a classificação não melhora. E a arbitragem? Sempre a mesma coisa...
nodes.npc_translation: Exactly, a disgrace! This way the league table won't improve. And the refereeing? Always the same thing...

options.text: Concordo plenamente. A arbitragem foi terrível.
options.translation: I completely agree. The refereeing was terrible.
options.feedback: Excellent, you're agreeing and adding your own strong opinion.

options.text: Não sei. Acho que o Marítimo tem de jogar melhor, independentemente da arbitragem.
options.translation: I don't know. I think Marítimo has to play better, regardless of the refereeing.
options.feedback: Good, you're offering a slightly different perspective, which is natural in conversation.

nodes.npc_text: Foi uma derrota, claro! Contra o último classificado. Não jogaram nada, uma desilusão para os adeptos. E a classificação está difícil.
nodes.npc_translation: It was a defeat, of course! Against the last-place team. They played terribly, a disappointment for the fans. And the league table is difficult.

options.text: Que pena! Espero que melhorem no próximo jogo.
options.translation: What a pity! I hope they improve in the next game.
options.feedback: Good, you're showing empathy and hope for the team.

options.text: Pois, isso complica a classificação, não é?
options.translation: Right, that complicates the league table, doesn't it?
options.feedback: Good, you're showing understanding of the implications.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é. Mas pronto, é o futebol. O que importa é a paixão pelo clube! Volte sempre!
nodes.npc_translation: Exactly. But oh well, that's football. What matters is the passion for the club! Come back anytime!

options.text: Obrigado! Até amanhã!
options.translation: Thank you! See you tomorrow!
options.feedback: Perfect, a polite and common way to end a brief interaction.

options.text: Até já! Espero que o próximo jogo seja melhor.
options.translation: See you soon! I hope the next game is better.
options.feedback: Excellent, you're ending politely and expressing a common sentiment.

mission.title: Talk Football with a Local
prep: Review the vocabulary for football results (vitória, derrota, empate) and opinions (jogou bem/mal, arbitragem).
Practice asking about a game: 'Viste o jogo do Marítimo/Nacional ontem?'
Practice expressing disappointment: 'Que pena!' or 'Que desilusão!'
Practice expressing agreement: 'Pois é!', 'Concordo.'
fallback_phrases: Desculpa, o meu português ainda é um bocado fraco.
likely_responses: Não, não vi. Como é que foi?

goals: Engage in small talk about local football teams and recent matches.
Express opinions and reactions (disappointment, joy, agreement) about game results and team performance.
Use common vocabulary related to football and league standings.
Understand and respond to typical comments about local sports in Madeira.

review_items.prompt: Say in Portuguese: How did {team} play yesterday?
Say in Portuguese: What a {feeling}!
Say in Portuguese: The league table is {state}.
Say in Portuguese: I think that {opinion}.
Recall the Portuguese for: league table / standings
Recall the Portuguese for: fan / supporter
Recall the Portuguese for: draw / tie
Recall the Portuguese for: victory
Listen to the dialogue at natural speed and answer: what is being asked for?

situations.title: Visiting the Villages
situations.summary: A day out to Câmara de Lobos, Santana or Porto Moniz: asking what to see, talking to locals about their village, and understanding proud local explanations.

phrase_patterns.base: O que há para ver em {village}?
phrase_patterns.translation: What is there to see in {village}?

variants.text: O que é que se pode fazer aqui?
variants.note: More general, asking about activities.
More emphatic, common in recommendations.

vocabulary.translation: irrigation channel / walk along a levada
vocabulary.note: Specific to Madeira, used for walks.
Popular alcoholic drink made with aguardente, honey, and lemon/orange.
Refers to the traditional triangular houses of Santana.
Volcanic rock pools, notably in Porto Moniz.

dialogues.title: Exploring Câmara de Lobos
dialogues.context: A visitor asks a local fisherman in Câmara de Lobos for recommendations.

lines.speaker: Visitor
lines.text: Bom dia! O que é que se pode ver aqui em Câmara de Lobos?
lines.translation: Good morning! What can one see here in Câmara de Lobos?

lines.speaker: Fisherman
lines.text: Ah, bom dia! Bem, devia ir ver o porto, claro. E depois, subir ao miradouro do Pico da Torre. Tem uma vista linda.
lines.translation: Ah, good morning! Well, you should go see the port, of course. And then, go up to the Pico da Torre viewpoint. It has a beautiful view.

lines.speaker: Visitor
lines.text: Pico da Torre, ok. E para comer, alguma sugestão?
lines.translation: Pico da Torre, ok. And for eating, any suggestion?

lines.speaker: Fisherman
lines.text: Pois, há muitos restaurantes bons de peixe. Experimente o peixe-espada preto. E claro, uma poncha!
lines.translation: Well, there are many good fish restaurants. Try the black scabbardfish. And of course, a poncha!

lines.speaker: Visitor
lines.text: Peixe-espada e poncha. Ótimas dicas, muito obrigado!
lines.translation: Scabbardfish and poncha. Great tips, thank you very much!

lines.speaker: Fisherman
lines.text: De nada! Aproveite a visita.
lines.translation: You're welcome! Enjoy your visit.

dialogues.title: Santana Trip Recap
dialogues.context: Two friends, Ana and Rui, are chatting about their recent trip to Santana.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Então, Rui, gostaste de Santana?
lines.translation: So, Rui, did you like Santana?

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Adorei! As casas típicas são mesmo giras, nunca tinha visto nada assim.
lines.translation: I loved it! The typical houses are really cool, I'd never seen anything like it.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Pois é, são únicas. E a paisagem? Foste dar umas voltas?
lines.translation: That's right, they're unique. And the landscape? Did you go for some walks?

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Fui! Fiz uma levada perto e é um sítio muito calmo. A natureza ali é incrível.
lines.translation: I did! I did a levada walk nearby and it's a very calm place. The nature there is incredible.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Que bom! É um dos meus sítios preferidos na ilha.
lines.translation: How nice! It's one of my favourite places on the island.

cultural_notes.title: Madeiran Pride in Local Villages
cultural_notes.body: Madeirans often have a strong sense of pride in their local villages and their unique characteristics. When you ask about things to see or do, locals are usually very happy and proud to share recommendations, often highlighting what makes their village special, be it natural beauty, historical sites, or local traditions like specific foods or drinks. Engaging with this pride can lead to very warm and informative interactions.

cultural_notes.title: Asking for Recommendations Politely
cultural_notes.body: When asking for recommendations, start with a polite greeting ('Bom dia', 'Boa tarde') and a clear, simple question like 'O que há para ver aqui?' (What is there to see here?) or 'O que é que se pode fazer?' (What can one do?). Listen attentively to their suggestions and express gratitude ('Muito obrigado/a', 'Ótimas dicas'). This shows respect and encourages helpful responses.

roleplay.scenario: You're in Porto Moniz, known for its natural pools. You approach a local resident sitting near the main square to ask for recommendations.

nodes.npc_text: Boa tarde! Posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Good afternoon! Can I help?

options.text: Boa tarde. O que há para ver aqui em Porto Moniz?
options.translation: Good afternoon. What is there to see here in Porto Moniz?
options.feedback: Great start! Direct and polite.

options.text: Olá. O que é que é giro para fazer por aqui?
options.translation: Hi. What's cool to do around here?
options.feedback: A bit more informal, but acceptable. 'Giro' means 'cool' or 'fun'.

nodes.npc_text: Ora bem, claro que tem as piscinas naturais, são o nosso maior orgulho! Mas também devia ir ao Aquário da Madeira e ver o farol, é muito bonito.
nodes.npc_translation: Well, of course, you have the natural pools, they are our greatest pride! But you should also go to the Madeira Aquarium and see the lighthouse, it's very beautiful.

options.text: As piscinas naturais são abertas o ano todo?
options.translation: Are the natural pools open all year round?
options.feedback: Good follow-up question, showing interest.

options.text: Obrigado! Onde é que é o Aquário?
options.translation: Thank you! Where is the Aquarium?
options.feedback: Asking for directions is practical and useful here.

nodes.npc_text: Sim, estão abertas, mas no inverno a água é mais fria, claro. Mas vale sempre a pena! E não perca o bolo do caco com manteiga de alho, é típico daqui.
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, they are open, but in winter the water is colder, of course. But it's always worth it! And don't miss the bolo do caco with garlic butter, it's typical here.

options.text: Bolo do caco? Que bom! Vou experimentar. Obrigado pelas dicas!
options.translation: Bolo do caco? How good! I'll try it. Thanks for the tips!
options.feedback: Excellent, you've shown appreciation and ended politely.

nodes.npc_text: O Aquário é ali, ao lado do Forte de São João Batista. É uma caminhada pequena. Gostou de Porto Moniz até agora?
nodes.npc_translation: The Aquarium is there, next to the São João Batista Fort. It's a short walk. Have you liked Porto Moniz so far?

options.text: Sim, estou a gostar imenso! É um sítio muito bonito. Obrigado pela ajuda!
options.translation: Yes, I'm liking it a lot! It's a very beautiful place. Thank you for your help!
options.feedback: Positive feedback and polite ending. Well done!

options.text: Sim, é muito agradável. Vou lá espreitar o Aquário. Muito obrigado!
options.translation: Yes, it's very pleasant. I'll go check out the Aquarium. Thank you very much!
options.feedback: Good, you're confirming your plans and thanking the person.

mission.title: Ask a Local for Village Tips
prep: Rehearse asking 'O que há para ver aqui em {village}?' or 'O que é que se pode fazer por aqui?'
Practice expressing interest with 'Que bom!' or 'Interessante!'
Prepare to thank the person with 'Muito obrigado/a pelas dicas!'
Familiarize yourself with local foods like 'bolo do caco' and 'poncha'.
likely_responses: Temos as piscinas naturais / as casas típicas...
Não perca a poncha / o bolo do caco!
Onde é que fica? É ali, perto de...

goals: Ask locals for recommendations on what to see and do in a Madeiran village.
Understand descriptions of local attractions and activities.
Express interest and gratitude in a natural, polite way.
Use key Madeiran vocabulary related to places and experiences.

review_items.prompt: Say in Portuguese: What is there to see in {village}?
review_items.answer: O que há para ver em {village}?

review_items.prompt: Say in Portuguese: You should really go to {place}.
Say in Portuguese: I really liked {activity}.
Say in Portuguese: It's a very {adjective} place.
Recall the Portuguese for: irrigation channel / walk along a levada
Recall the Portuguese for: traditional Madeiran drink
Recall the Portuguese for: viewpoint
Recall the Portuguese for: typical houses
Listen to the dialogue at natural speed and answer: what is being asked for?

situations.title: Getting Around the Island
situations.summary: Island transport talk: the rodoviária buses to the villages, asking drivers about times and stops, taxis for the return, and the mountain roads (curvas, túneis) as small talk.

phrase_patterns.base: O autocarro para {destino} sai a que horas?
phrase_patterns.translation: What time does the bus to {destination} leave?

variants.text: A que horas sai o autocarro para {destino}?
variants.translation: What time does the bus to {destination} leave?

phrase_patterns.base: Queria um bilhete de {tipo} para {destino}, por favor.
phrase_patterns.translation: I'd like a {type} ticket to {destination}, please.
Does this bus go to the {bus_stop}?

vocabulary.note: Common term in Portugal for a bus terminal

vocabulary.translation: irrigation channel; walking path alongside it
vocabulary.note: Specific to Madeira

dialogues.title: At the Bus Station
dialogues.context: The learner is at the Funchal bus station (rodoviária) asking a ticket agent about a bus to Ribeiro Frio.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Bom dia! Queria saber a que horas sai o autocarro para o Ribeiro Frio, por favor.
lines.translation: Good morning! I'd like to know what time the bus to Ribeiro Frio leaves, please.

lines.speaker: Agent
lines.text: Bom dia. O próximo para o Ribeiro Frio é às dez e quinze. É um bilhete de ida e volta?
lines.translation: Good morning. The next one to Ribeiro Frio is at 10:15. Is it a return ticket?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Sim, um bilhete de ida e volta, por favor. Quanto é?
lines.translation: Yes, a return ticket, please. How much is it?

lines.speaker: Agent
lines.text: Fica em cinco euros e vinte. O autocarro sai da plataforma três.
lines.translation: It's five euros and twenty cents. The bus leaves from platform three.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Muito obrigado! Até já.
lines.translation: Thank you very much! See you later.

dialogues.title: On the Bus to a Village
dialogues.context: The learner is on a bus, talking to an older local about the journey and the island's roads.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Desculpe, este autocarro vai até à paragem da igreja em Santana?
lines.translation: Excuse me, does this bus go to the church stop in Santana?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Pois vai, sim. Ainda faltam umas quantas paragens, mas chega lá. É a sua primeira vez em Santana?
lines.translation: Yes, it does. There are still a few stops, but it gets there. Is it your first time in Santana?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: É sim. As estradas aqui são mesmo cheias de curvas e túneis, não é?
lines.translation: Yes, it is. The roads here are really full of curves and tunnels, aren't they?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Ah, pois são! Mas agora tá bem melhor, com os túneis novos. Antigamente é que era uma aventura, com as estradas antigas! Mas tem paisagens lindas, não tem?
lines.translation: Oh, yes they are! But now it's much better, with the new tunnels. Back then it was an adventure, with the old roads! But it has beautiful landscapes, doesn't it?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Sim, são fantásticas. Estou a gostar imenso da ilha.
lines.translation: Yes, they are fantastic. I'm really enjoying the island a lot.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Ainda bem! Fico feliz por ouvir isso. Veja bem as casas típicas em Santana.
lines.translation: That's good! I'm happy to hear that. Make sure to see the typical houses in Santana.

cultural_notes.title: Bus Travel in Madeira (Horários e Paragens)
cultural_notes.body: Buses (autocarros) are a primary way to get around Madeira, especially to smaller villages. Schedules (horários) can be less frequent on weekends or to remote areas, so it's always good to check in advance. When on the bus, you might need to press a button to signal your stop (paragem), or simply ask the driver (motorista) if you're unsure. Drivers are generally helpful, especially if you're polite.

cultural_notes.title: Small Talk about Madeiran Roads
cultural_notes.body: Madeira is known for its steep, winding roads (curvas) and numerous tunnels (túneis) that cut through the mountains. Locals often chat about the roads, comparing the old, more challenging routes with the newer, faster ones. It's a common and safe topic for conversation, showing appreciation for the island's unique geography and infrastructure.

roleplay.scenario: You're at a bus stop in a small village (e.g., São Vicente) and want to get back to Funchal. You see a local waiting and decide to ask for help with the bus schedule.

nodes.npc_text: Boa tarde! O autocarro para o Funchal deve estar quase a chegar.
nodes.npc_translation: Good afternoon! The bus to Funchal should be arriving soon.

options.text: Boa tarde. Sabe a que horas é que passa o próximo autocarro para o Funchal?
options.translation: Good afternoon. Do you know what time the next bus to Funchal passes?
options.feedback: A polite way to ask for the schedule.

options.text: Olá. Este autocarro vai para o Funchal?
options.translation: Hi. Does this bus go to Funchal?
options.feedback: A direct question, but you could be more specific about the time.

nodes.npc_text: Pois, o próximo é daqui a uns dez minutos, às quatro e meia. Depois há outro só lá para as seis. Vai para o Funchal?
nodes.npc_translation: Well, the next one is in about ten minutes, at half past four. Then there's another one only around six. Are you going to Funchal?

options.text: Sim, vou. Muito obrigado pela informação. É a minha primeira vez por aqui.
options.translation: Yes, I am. Thank you very much for the information. It's my first time around here.
options.feedback: Show appreciation and give a little context.

options.text: Sim. É muito tempo de viagem até lá?
options.translation: Yes. Is it a long journey there?
options.feedback: Asking about the duration of the trip.

nodes.npc_text: Sim, vai. É o das quatro e meia. O próximo só é lá para as seis. Vai para o Funchal?
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, it does. It's the 4:30 one. The next one is only around six. Are you going to Funchal?

options.text: Sim, vou. É a primeira vez que ando de autocarro por aqui. As estradas são um bocado sinuosas, não é?
options.translation: Yes, I am. It's my first time taking the bus here. The roads are a bit winding, aren't they?
options.feedback: Confirm your destination and make small talk about the roads.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, pois é! As estradas são bonitas, mas têm muitas curvas e túneis. É a beleza da ilha, não é? De onde é que veio?
nodes.npc_translation: Oh, yes they are! The roads are beautiful, but they have many curves and tunnels. It's the beauty of the island, isn't it? Where are you from?

options.text: Sou de fora, estou cá de férias. Estou a adorar a Madeira!
options.translation: I'm from abroad, I'm here on holiday. I'm loving Madeira!
options.feedback: A common and positive response.

nodes.npc_text: Olhe, até ao Funchal é cerca de uma hora e pouco, com as paragens. Não é mau. Vai para alguma levada por aqui?
nodes.npc_translation: Look, it's about an hour and a bit to Funchal, with the stops. It's not bad. Are you going to any levada around here?

options.text: Não, hoje não. Só queria visitar a zona. Mas já fiz algumas levadas, são incríveis!
options.translation: No, not today. I just wanted to visit the area. But I've already done some levadas, they're incredible!
options.feedback: Answer the question and share your positive experience with levadas.

nodes.npc_text: Ainda bem! A ilha é linda. Ah, o autocarro está a chegar! Tenha uma boa viagem de regresso ao Funchal.
nodes.npc_translation: That's good! The island is beautiful. Oh, the bus is arriving! Have a good trip back to Funchal.

options.text: Muito obrigado! Bom resto de dia.
options.translation: Thank you very much! Have a good rest of the day.
options.feedback: A polite way to end the conversation as the bus arrives.

mission.title: Navigating the Island Bus System
prep: Review the vocabulary for transport and directions.
Practice asking for bus times and destinations.
Rehearse making small talk about Madeiran roads.
fallback_phrases: Pode falar mais devagar, por favor?

Understand information about bus schedules and routes.

Politely ask a bus driver to stop at your desired location.

situations.title: Month 3 Grand Stress Test
situations.summary: Island-life conversations combined: a levada encounter, café counter talk, produce and football banter, and an errand — month 3 at natural speed.

phrase_patterns.base: O que é que achas da {assunto}?
phrase_patterns.translation: What do you think about the {topic}?

variants.text: O que é que achas do {assunto}?
variants.translation: What do you think about the {topic}?
variants.note: Use 'do' for masculine nouns, 'da' for feminine nouns

dialogues.title: Levada Encounter
dialogues.context: Two neighbours, Maria and João, meet unexpectedly while walking on a levada path in Madeira.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Olá, João! Tudo bem? Que surpresa encontrá-lo aqui!
lines.translation: Hello, João! All good? What a surprise to find you here!

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Olá, Maria! Tudo bem, e contigo? Pois é, vim dar um passeio p'ra apanhar um bocado de ar fresco.
lines.translation: Hello, Maria! All good, and with you? That's right, I came for a walk to get a bit of fresh air.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Está um dia tão bonito, não está? A vista daqui é sempre incrível.
lines.translation: It's such a beautiful day, isn't it? The view from here is always incredible.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Pois é, está bom para caminhar. E a tua família, tudo bem?
lines.translation: That's right, it's good for walking. And your family, all good?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Está tudo bem, obrigada. O meu neto está a jogar futebol hoje, lá no campo.
lines.translation: All good, thank you. My grandson is playing football today, there at the field.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Ah, sim? Espero que ganhem! Eu tenho de ir p'ra casa, o almoço está à espera. Vemo-nos por aí!
lines.translation: Oh, really? I hope they win! I have to go home, lunch is waiting. See you around!

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Até já, João! Bom almoço!
lines.translation: See you later, João! Enjoy your lunch!

dialogues.title: Café and Market Errands
dialogues.context: The learner is at a local café, then goes to a small market stall to buy produce.

lines.speaker: Balconista
lines.text: Diga! O que vai ser?
lines.translation: Tell me! What will it be?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Queria uma bica e um pastel de nata, por favor.
lines.translation: I'd like an espresso and a custard tart, please.

lines.speaker: Balconista
lines.text: P'ra já! Aqui está. São dois euros e vinte.
lines.translation: Coming right up! Here it is. That's two euros and twenty.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Obrigado. Está muito bom!
lines.translation: Thank you. It's very good!

lines.speaker: Vendedora
lines.text: Olá! Posso ajudar?
lines.translation: Hello! Can I help you?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Sim, queria um quilo de semilhas e umas bananas da Madeira, por favor.
lines.translation: Yes, I'd like a kilo of potatoes and some Madeiran bananas, please.

lines.speaker: Vendedora
lines.text: Claro! As bananas estão muito boas hoje. Quer mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: Of course! The bananas are very good today. Do you want anything else?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Não, é tudo. Quanto é?
lines.translation: No, that's all. How much is it?

lines.speaker: Vendedora
lines.text: São três euros e cinquenta. Volte sempre!
lines.translation: That's three euros and fifty. Come back anytime!

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Portuguese Shops
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, it's customary to use 'por favor' (please) when asking for something and 'obrigado/a' (thank you) when receiving it, even for small transactions. Shopkeepers often use 'Diga!' (Tell me!) or 'O que vai ser?' (What will it be?) to invite your order. When leaving, 'Volte sempre!' (Come back always!) is a common, friendly farewell.

cultural_notes.title: Madeiran Regionalisms for Food
cultural_notes.body: While 'batata' is the standard Portuguese word for potato, in Madeira, 'semilha' is very commonly used. Similarly, 'banana da Madeira' specifies the local, smaller, and sweeter variety of banana grown on the island, which is highly prized.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a local talho (butcher shop) in Madeira, wanting to buy some meat for an espetada.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Em que posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! How can I help?

options.text: Bom dia! Queria carne para fazer espetada, por favor.
options.translation: Good morning! I'd like meat to make espetada, please.
options.feedback: Great start! You specified the type of meat and the dish.

options.text: Queria carne de vaca.
options.translation: I'd like beef.
options.feedback: Good, but remember to add 'por favor' for politeness.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, espetada! Boa escolha. Quantos quilos precisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, espetada! Good choice. How many kilos do you need?

options.text: Um quilo e meio, por favor.
options.translation: One and a half kilos, please.
options.feedback: Excellent, clear quantity with politeness.

options.text: Para quatro pessoas, se faz favor.
options.translation: For four people, please.
options.feedback: Good, indicating quantity by number of people is natural.

nodes.npc_text: Carne de vaca, sim. Para que prato? Para estufar, grelhar ou espetada?
nodes.npc_translation: Beef, yes. For what dish? For stewing, grilling, or espetada?

options.text: É para fazer espetada, por favor.
options.translation: It's to make espetada, please.
options.feedback: Good, you clarified your request.

nodes.npc_text: Certo. E mais alguma coisa? Temos umas semilhas muito boas hoje.
nodes.npc_translation: Alright. And anything else? We have some very good potatoes today.

options.text: Não, é tudo. Quanto é?
options.translation: No, that's all. How much is it?
options.feedback: Perfect, asking for the total. Remember 'é tudo' is common.

options.text: Sim, queria também um quilo de semilhas, por favor.
options.translation: Yes, I'd also like a kilo of potatoes, please.
options.feedback: Good, you added another item to your order.

nodes.npc_text: Deixe ver... a carne fica a doze euros e cinquenta. São doze euros e cinquenta no total.
nodes.npc_translation: Let me see... the meat is twelve euros and fifty. That's twelve euros and fifty in total.

options.text: Aqui está. Obrigado!
options.translation: Here you go. Thank you!
options.feedback: You successfully completed your purchase! Well done.

nodes.npc_text: Um quilo de semilhas. Então, a carne e as semilhas ficam a quatorze euros e oitenta no total.
nodes.npc_translation: A kilo of potatoes. So, the meat and potatoes are fourteen euros and eighty in total.

options.text: Aqui está o dinheiro. Muito obrigado!
options.translation: Here's the money. Thank you very much!
options.feedback: Excellent! You handled the transaction smoothly, including the extra item.

situations.title: Neighbourhood Small Talk
situations.summary: Chatting with a neighbour on the street: weather, the levada, how things are going — the daily social glue of belonging in a Madeira bairro.

variants.note: A common, more casual greeting.

phrase_patterns.base: Fui dar um passeio na levada de {levada_name}.
phrase_patterns.translation: I went for a walk on the {levada_name} levada.

variants.text: Gosto imenso de caminhar nas levadas.
variants.translation: I really like walking on the levadas.

phrase_patterns.base: Pois é, a vida aqui é {how_life_is}, não é?
phrase_patterns.translation: That's right, life here is {how_life_is}, isn't it?

vocabulary.note: Specific to Madeira, these channels also serve as popular walking trails.
Traditional Madeiran drink made with aguardente de cana, honey, and lemon juice.
A traditional Madeiran bread, often served with garlic butter.
Regional term for 'potato' in Madeira; in mainland Portugal, 'batata'.

Used for agreement or acknowledgement in conversation.
Commonly used in Portugal instead of 'muito' for emphasis.
Used to mean 'a little' or 'a bit'.

dialogues.title: Morning Chat on the Street
dialogues.context: Dona Lúcia and Marco, neighbours, meet on the street near a small grocery store in the morning.

lines.speaker: Dona Lúcia
lines.text: Bom dia, Marco! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Good morning, Marco! All good?

lines.speaker: Marco
lines.text: Bom dia, Dona Lúcia! Sim, tudo bem, obrigado. E a senhora, como está?
lines.translation: Good morning, Dona Lúcia! Yes, all good, thank you. And you, how are you?

lines.speaker: Dona Lúcia
lines.text: Está-se bem, está-se bem. Este solzinho já ajuda, não é? Ontem esteve um bocado esquisito.
lines.translation: I'm well, I'm well. This little sun already helps, doesn't it? Yesterday it was a bit strange.

lines.speaker: Marco
lines.text: Pois é, o tempo aqui muda num instante. A senhora vai às compras?
lines.translation: That's right, the weather here changes in an instant. Are you going shopping?

lines.speaker: Dona Lúcia
lines.text: Vou sim, preciso de umas semilhas para o jantar. E o Marco, já foi à levada hoje?
lines.translation: Yes, I am, I need some potatoes for dinner. And Marco, have you been to the levada today?

lines.speaker: Marco
lines.text: Ainda não, mas estou a planear ir à tarde. A de São Jorge é sempre bonita nesta altura.
lines.translation: Not yet, but I'm planning to go this afternoon. The São Jorge one is always beautiful at this time of year.

lines.speaker: Dona Lúcia
lines.text: É verdade. Olhe, tenho de ir, que as semilhas não se compram sozinhas! Até logo, Marco!
lines.translation: It's true. Look, I have to go, the potatoes don't buy themselves! See you later, Marco!

lines.speaker: Marco
lines.text: Até logo, Dona Lúcia! Bom dia!
lines.translation: See you later, Dona Lúcia! Good day!

cultural_notes.title: The Importance of Greetings and Small Talk
cultural_notes.body: In Madeiran communities, it's very common and expected to greet neighbours and engage in brief small talk when you meet them on the street or in local shops. This daily interaction is a vital part of social cohesion and belonging. Even a simple 'Bom dia!' or 'Tudo bem?' followed by a quick comment about the weather or daily plans helps maintain community bonds.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Pois é' for Agreement
cultural_notes.body: 'Pois é' is a very common expression in European Portuguese, especially in informal settings like neighbourhood chats. It's used to express agreement, acknowledgement, or to confirm something that has just been said. It can be translated as 'that's right', 'indeed', or 'it is so'.

roleplay.scenario: You meet your neighbour, Senhor Manuel, near the local minimarket in the morning. He's a friendly older man who enjoys a quick chat.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Como é que está? Está um dia bonito, não está?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! How are you? It's a beautiful day, isn't it?

options.text: Bom dia, Senhor Manuel! Estou bem, obrigado. Sim, está um dia ótimo!
options.translation: Good morning, Senhor Manuel! I'm well, thank you. Yes, it's a great day!
options.feedback: Great start! You greeted him back and agreed about the weather politely.

options.text: Olá! Tudo bem. O tempo está bom hoje.
options.translation: Hello! All good. The weather is good today.
options.feedback: Good, a slightly more informal but perfectly acceptable response.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é, um dia bom para ir à levada. Eu já fui, à levada do Rei. E o que vai fazer hoje?
nodes.npc_translation: That's right, a good day to go to the levada. I've already been, to the Levada do Rei. And what are you going to do today?

options.text: Que bom! Eu ainda não fui, mas talvez vá à tarde. Gosto imenso da de São Jorge.
options.translation: How nice! I haven't been yet, but maybe I'll go this afternoon. I really like the São Jorge one.
options.feedback: Excellent, you shared your plans and mentioned a local levada.

options.text: Ah, a levada do Rei é muito bonita! Eu hoje vou só tratar de umas coisas em casa.
options.translation: Ah, the Levada do Rei is very beautiful! Today I'm just going to take care of some things at home.
options.feedback: Good, you acknowledged his activity and shared your own simple plans.

nodes.npc_text: Sim, todas as levadas têm o seu encanto. Olhe, preciso de ir comprar um bolo do caco para o almoço. A gente encontra-se por aí!
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, all the levadas have their charm. Look, I need to go buy a bolo do caco for lunch. We'll see each other around!

options.text: Pois é! Até logo, Senhor Manuel! Bom almoço!
options.translation: That's right! See you later, Senhor Manuel! Have a good lunch!
options.feedback: Perfect! You acknowledged his comment and said a friendly goodbye.

options.text: Sim, concordo. Até logo!
options.translation: Yes, I agree. See you later!
options.feedback: Good, a simple and polite way to end the conversation.

situations.summary: Enjoying a Madeiran arraial: buying espetada and poncha, joining the crowd, understanding festival announcements and invitations.

variants.note: Slightly less formal

dialogues.title: Ordering at the Arraial
dialogues.context: At a busy food stall during a Madeiran arraial, a local is ordering from a vendor.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Boa noite! O que vai ser?
lines.translation: Good evening! What will it be?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Boa noite! Queria uma espetada e um bolo do caco, por favor.
lines.translation: Good evening! I'd like a beef skewer and a bolo do caco, please.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Com ou sem semilhas?
lines.translation: With or without potatoes?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Ah, sem semilhas, por enquanto. E uma poncha, pode ser?
lines.translation: Ah, without potatoes for now. And a poncha, if possible?

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Claro! Fica tudo dezasseis euros. É p'ra levar ou come aqui?
lines.translation: Of course! That's sixteen euros. Is it to go or are you eating here?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Não, é p'ra comer aqui, no meio da confusão!
lines.translation: No, it's to eat here, in the middle of the hustle and bustle!

dialogues.title: Enjoying the Arraial
dialogues.context: Two friends, Ana and Rui, are trying to find a spot to eat their food at a crowded arraial. The general ambience is noisy.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Ufa, que confusão! Mas a espetada está ótima, não está?
lines.translation: Phew, what a crowd! But the espetada is great, isn't it?

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Está uma delícia! E esta poncha... humm! Precisava mesmo disto.
lines.translation: It's delicious! And this poncha... mmm! I really needed this.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Pois é. A animação também está boa este ano. Estão a tocar umas músicas fixes.
lines.translation: Indeed. The entertainment is also good this year. They're playing some cool songs.

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Sim, sim! Olha, queres ir ver as barraquinhas de artesanato depois? São por ali.
lines.translation: Yes, yes! Look, do you want to go see the craft stalls later? They're over there.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Diz? Não ouvi bem! As barraquinhas de quê?
lines.translation: What? I didn't hear well! The stalls of what?

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: De artesanato! P'ra ver se há alguma coisa gira.
lines.translation: Crafts! To see if there's anything nice.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Ah, sim! Boa ideia. Mas primeiro, mais um bocado de espetada!
lines.translation: Ah, yes! Good idea. But first, a bit more espetada!

cultural_notes.title: The Madeiran Arraial
cultural_notes.body: Arraiais are traditional religious festivals in Madeira, often celebrating a patron saint. While they have a strong religious component (masses, processions), they are also major social events. Expect lively music, dancing, and, most importantly, abundant food and drink stalls. They are a fantastic way to experience Madeiran culture and gastronomy. The atmosphere is usually very informal and joyful.

cultural_notes.title: Ordering Food and Drink
cultural_notes.body: At an arraial, food and drink are typically ordered at 'barracas' (stalls). It's common to queue up, place your order, pay, and then find a spot to eat, often standing or at shared tables. Don't be shy to ask 'Com licença' to navigate through crowds. Using 'por favor' and 'obrigado/a' is always polite, even in a bustling environment. Madeirans often use 'semilha' for potato instead of 'batata'.

roleplay.scenario: You're at a busy arraial in Madeira, feeling hungry and thirsty. You decide to approach a food stall to order some typical Madeiran food and a drink.

nodes.npc_text: Boa noite! O que vai ser? Temos espetada, bolo do caco, e semilhas fritas!
nodes.npc_translation: Good evening! What will it be? We have espetada, bolo do caco, and fried potatoes!

options.text: Queria uma espetada, por favor.
options.translation: I'd like an espetada, please.
options.feedback: Great start! You clearly asked for the espetada. Now, what about a drink?

options.text: Olá! Quanto é que custa a espetada?
options.translation: Hello! How much does the espetada cost?
options.feedback: Good, you asked about the price first. Now you can decide to order.

nodes.npc_text: A espetada é dez euros. As semilhas são três. E as bebidas, temos poncha e água.
nodes.npc_translation: The espetada is ten euros. The potatoes are three. And for drinks, we have poncha and water.

options.text: Ok, queria uma espetada e uma poncha, por favor.
options.translation: Okay, I'd like an espetada and a poncha, please.
options.feedback: Excellent! You ordered both food and drink.

options.text: Só a espetada, então. E uma água, se faz favor.
options.translation: Just the espetada, then. And a water, please.
options.feedback: Good choice, you opted for water. Now, time to pay.

nodes.npc_text: Certo. E p'ra beber? Uma poncha, talvez?
nodes.npc_translation: Alright. And to drink? A poncha, perhaps?

options.text: Sim, uma poncha, por favor.
options.translation: Yes, a poncha, please.
options.feedback: Perfeito! You've ordered both your meal and a typical Madeiran drink.

options.text: Não, obrigada. Só a espetada.
options.translation: No, thank you. Just the espetada.
options.feedback: That's fine, you politely declined the drink. Now to the payment.

nodes.npc_text: Fica tudo doze euros e cinquenta. É p'ra levar ou come aqui?
nodes.npc_translation: That's twelve euros fifty. Is it to go or are you eating here?

options.text: É p'ra comer aqui, obrigada.
options.translation: It's to eat here, thank you.
options.feedback: Good, you specified you're eating there. Make sure you pay!

options.text: É p'ra levar, por favor.
options.translation: It's to go, please.
options.feedback: Excellent, you chose to take it away. Now, pay up!

nodes.npc_text: Aqui está! Bom apetite!
nodes.npc_translation: Here you go! Enjoy your meal!

options.text: Obrigada! Está muito bom!
options.translation: Thank you! It's very good!
options.feedback: Great, you expressed your appreciation for the food. Well done!

options.text: Obrigada! Com licença, posso passar?
options.translation: Thank you! Excuse me, may I pass?
options.feedback: Perfect! You thanked the vendor and politely asked to navigate the crowd. Mission accomplished!

situations.title: Madeiran Expressions & Humour
situations.summary: Understanding and using local turns of phrase and gentle humour so conversation feels natural, not textbook.

variants.text: Está a cair uma chuvinha miúda.
variants.note: A more descriptive, informal variant.

phrase_patterns.translation: It's from {the origin}. / It's a local {thing/person}.

variants.note: A more general way to say it's local.

vocabulary.note: Reflexive verb.

dialogues.title: A Morning Chat in the Village
dialogues.context: Maria and João, neighbours in a small Madeiran village, meet by chance near the local café. It's a slightly grey morning.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Bom dia, João! Que tempo, não é? Parece que está a chover miúdo.
lines.translation: Good morning, João! What weather, isn't it? It looks like it's drizzling lightly.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Bom dia, Maria! Pois é, mas é da Madeira. Nunca se sabe! Vais tomar uma bica?
lines.translation: Good morning, Maria! Indeed, but it's from Madeira. You never know! Are you going for a coffee?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Vou sim. Tenho de ir ao mercado depois, mas com esta chuvinha... que maçada!
lines.translation: Yes, I am. I have to go to the market afterwards, but with this drizzle... what a nuisance!

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Ah, não te acanhes! É só uma chuvinha. O mercado está coberto. Eu já lá vou também.
lines.translation: Ah, don't be shy! It's just a little rain. The market is covered. I'm going there too.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Tens razão. Então, até já no mercado!
lines.translation: You're right. So, see you at the market!

cultural_notes.title: Madeiran Weather Talk: 'Chover Miúdo'
cultural_notes.body: 'Está a chover miúdo' is a very common and specific Madeiran expression for light, fine rain or drizzle. While 'chuviscar' is used across Portugal, 'chover miúdo' is a local favourite. It reflects the island's often unpredictable and varied microclimates.

cultural_notes.title: Encouragement and Hospitality: 'Não te acanhes!'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, and especially in Madeira, it's common to encourage guests or friends to feel at home and not hold back, particularly when offering food or drink. 'Não te acanhes!' is a warm, informal way to say 'Don't be shy!' or 'Help yourself!', showing genuine hospitality and a desire for you to relax and enjoy.

roleplay.scenario: You're at a small local market stall in Madeira, looking at some fresh fruit. The vendor notices you and offers you a taste of some local produce.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Gostavas de provar esta anona? É da terra, muito fresquinha.
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Would you like to try this custard apple? It's local, very fresh.

options.text: Sim, obrigado/a! Parece deliciosa.
options.translation: Yes, thank you! It looks delicious.
options.feedback: Great! You accepted politely and showed enthusiasm. This is a common and welcome response.

options.text: Não, obrigado/a. Só estou a olhar.
options.translation: No, thank you. I'm just looking.
options.feedback: This is polite, but you missed an opportunity to engage with the local culture and hospitality. Vendors often appreciate you trying their produce.

nodes.npc_text: Então, não te acanhes! É mesmo boa. Diz lá o que achas.
nodes.npc_translation: So, don't be shy! It's really good. Tell me what you think.

options.text: Mmm, é fantástica! Muito doce.
options.translation: Mmm, it's fantastic! Very sweet.
options.feedback: Excellent! You tried the fruit and gave positive feedback, engaging naturally with the vendor.

options.text: Obrigado/a pela provinha.
options.translation: Thank you for the taste.
options.feedback: Good, you thanked them. Giving a short opinion ('muito bom', 'delicioso') would be even more engaging.

nodes.npc_text: Pois, como quiseres. Mas se mudares de ideias, é só dizer!
nodes.npc_translation: Well, as you wish. But if you change your mind, just say so!

options.text: Está bem, obrigado/a!
options.translation: Okay, thank you!
options.feedback: You ended the interaction politely. Next time, consider accepting the offer to engage more with local hospitality.

nodes.npc_text: Ainda bem que gostaste! Queres levar uma?
nodes.npc_translation: Glad you liked it! Do you want to take one?

options.text: Sim, por favor! Vou levar duas.
options.translation: Yes, please! I'll take two.
options.feedback: Perfect! You've successfully engaged, appreciated the local produce, and made a purchase.

options.text: Talvez para a próxima. Obrigado/a!
options.translation: Maybe next time. Thank you!
options.feedback: Polite, but a missed opportunity to buy after enjoying the sample. Vendors appreciate it when you buy after tasting.

mission.title: Embrace Madeiran Hospitality
prep: Practice saying 'Não te acanhes!' and understanding its context.
Rehearse how to accept and politely decline offers of food or drink, and how to give a simple opinion.
Listen for 'É da Madeira' or 'É da terra' to identify local products or customs.

goals: Understand and use the Madeiran expression 'chover miúdo' for light rain.
Respond appropriately to offers using 'Não te acanhes!' or similar encouragement.
Identify and use phrases related to local origin ('É da Madeira', 'É da terra').
Express mild annoyance or sympathy with 'Que maçada!'.
Engage in short, natural conversations with locals, showing appreciation for local customs and products.

Say in Portuguese: Don't be shy! / Don't hold back!
Say in Portuguese: What a nuisance! / What a pain!

situations.title: Month 4 Stress Test
situations.summary: Mixed real-time social conversation: neighbours, festival, opinions and local expressions combined under light pressure.

phrase_patterns.base: Eu acho que {evento} é muito bom.
phrase_patterns.translation: I think that {event} is very good.

variants.note: More enthusiastic informal opinion

phrase_patterns.base: Então, o que é que achas de {assunto}?
phrase_patterns.translation: So, what do you think of {topic}?

vocabulary.note: Common term for traditional village festivals in Madeira and Portugal
Madeiran regionalism, made with aguardente, honey, and lemon/orange juice
Madeiran regionalism, unique to the island's landscape
Madeiran regionalism for 'batata'
Common in Portugal, similar to 'muito'
Common in Portugal, similar to 'um pouco'
Used for agreement, confirmation, or as a filler word in conversation

dialogues.title: Festival Talk
dialogues.context: Two neighbours, Sofia and João, meet by chance at the local market, discussing the upcoming arraial.

lines.speaker: Sofia
lines.text: Bom dia, João! Tudo bem? Estás a ver as semilhas?
lines.translation: Good morning, João! All good? Are you looking at the potatoes?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Bom dia, Sofia! Sim, estão com bom aspeto. E tu, já a pensar no arraial do próximo fim de semana?
lines.translation: Good morning, Sofia! Yes, they look good. And you, already thinking about next weekend's festival?

lines.speaker: Sofia
lines.text: Pois é! Estou com imensa vontade de ir. A música ao vivo é sempre boa.
lines.translation: Indeed! I'm really looking forward to going. The live music is always good.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: É verdade. E a poncha, claro! Que tal irmos juntos, se não tiveres planos?
lines.translation: It's true. And the poncha, of course! How about we go together, if you don't have plans?

lines.speaker: Sofia
lines.text: Era ótimo! Eu acho que o arraial é sempre o melhor para animar. Vamos então?
lines.translation: That would be great! I think the festival is always the best to liven things up. Shall we go then?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Combinado. Falamos durante a semana. Até já!
lines.translation: It's a plan. We'll talk during the week. See you!

cultural_notes.title: Arraiais: The Heart of Madeiran Community
cultural_notes.body: Arraiais are traditional local festivals, often linked to religious celebrations, but they are also vibrant social events. They typically feature live music, traditional food (like espetadas and bolo do caco), drinks (especially poncha), and a lively atmosphere. They are a cornerstone of community life in Madeira, bringing neighbours together for fun and celebration. Expressing enthusiasm for an arraial is a common way to connect with locals.

cultural_notes.title: Expressing Opinions and Making Plans Informally
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, especially with neighbours or friends, conversations are often informal. Using 'tu' is standard. Phrases like 'Eu acho que...' (I think that...) are common for sharing opinions. When suggesting plans, 'Que tal irmos...?' (How about we go...?) is a friendly way to invite someone. Interjections like 'Pois é!' (Indeed!/That's right!) are used to show agreement and keep the conversation flowing naturally.

roleplay.scenario: You're at a local café, enjoying a bica. Your neighbour, Maria, approaches you and starts a conversation about the weekend and a local event.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Tudo bem? A aproveitar a bica? Já a pensar nos planos para o fim de semana?
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! All good? Enjoying your espresso? Already thinking about plans for the weekend?

options.text: Olá, Maria! Sim, está tudo bem. Ainda não pensei muito, e tu?
options.translation: Hello, Maria! Yes, all good. I haven't thought much yet, and you?
options.feedback: Good start! You greeted her and turned the question back to her.

options.text: Olá! Estou um bocado cansado, mas sim, a bica ajuda. Ainda não sei os planos.
options.translation: Hello! I'm a bit tired, but yes, the espresso helps. I don't know the plans yet.
options.feedback: It's fine to mention being tired. You still answered her question about plans.

nodes.npc_text: Eu também não. Mas ouvi dizer que vai haver um arraial pequeno na freguesia vizinha. Que tal irmos lá no sábado à noite?
nodes.npc_translation: Me neither. But I heard there's going to be a small festival in the next parish. How about we go there on Saturday night?

options.text: Um arraial? Que boa ideia! Eu acho que o arraial é sempre divertido.
options.translation: A festival? What a good idea! I think the festival is always fun.
options.feedback: Great! You showed enthusiasm and used a relevant phrase pattern.

options.text: Não sei. Estou um bocado cansado com a confusão dos arraiais.
options.translation: I don't know. I'm a bit tired with the crowds at festivals.
options.feedback: It's okay to express hesitation. Maria might try to convince you.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, pois é! O trabalho cansa. Mas ouvi dizer que vai haver um arraial pequeno na freguesia vizinha. Que tal irmos lá no sábado à noite? Talvez ajude a descontrair.
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, indeed! Work is tiring. But I heard there's going to be a small festival in the next parish. How about we go there on Saturday night? Maybe it will help you relax.

options.text: Pois, se calhar. Eu acho que a música ao vivo é boa para esquecer o cansaço.
options.translation: Yes, maybe. I think live music is good for forgetting tiredness.
options.feedback: Good, you're considering it and linking it to a positive aspect.

options.text: Não sei, Maria. Estou mesmo sem vontade de ir a sítios com muita gente.
options.translation: I don't know, Maria. I really don't feel like going to places with many people.
options.feedback: You're politely declining. That's a valid option in real life.

nodes.npc_text: Sim, a música e a poncha! É sempre bom. Então, combinado? Encontramo-nos à entrada às nove?
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, the music and the poncha! It's always good. So, it's a plan? Shall we meet at the entrance at nine?

options.text: Sim, combinado! Até sábado então. Obrigada pelo convite!
options.translation: Yes, it's a plan! See you Saturday then. Thanks for the invitation!
options.feedback: Excellent! You confirmed the plan and thanked her politely. Conversation ends well.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, mas este é mais pequeno, não tem tanta gente como os grandes. E a poncha é caseira! Pensa bem.
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, but this one is smaller, it doesn't have as many people as the big ones. And the poncha is homemade! Think about it.

options.text: Hmm, poncha caseira... Isso já me convence mais. Que tal irmos então?
options.translation: Hmm, homemade poncha... That convinces me more. How about we go then?
options.feedback: You're swayed by the details! Good, you're engaging with her arguments.

options.text: Mesmo assim, estou a precisar de um fim de semana mais calmo. Fica para a próxima.
options.translation: Even so, I need a calmer weekend. Maybe next time.
options.feedback: Polite refusal. It's good to know how to decline an invitation.

nodes.npc_text: Percebo perfeitamente. Às vezes precisamos de paz. Então, fica para a próxima. Mas se mudares de ideias, é só dizer!
nodes.npc_translation: I understand perfectly. Sometimes we need peace. So, next time then. But if you change your mind, just say so!

options.text: Obrigada, Maria! Bom arraial para ti. Até breve!
options.translation: Thanks, Maria! Have a good festival. See you soon!
options.feedback: You ended the conversation politely, wishing her well. Well done.

Understand and use common Madeiran expressions like 'arraial', 'poncha', 'um bocado', and 'pois'.

situations.title: Poncha Night: Rounds & Toasts
situations.summary: An evening at the poncha bar: ordering rounds, who pays (deixa, pago eu), simple toasts, refusing another one politely, and the social code of the taberna.

phrase_patterns.base: Mais uma rodada de {bebida}, se faz favor.

variants.text: Outra rodada de {bebida}, se faz favor.
Não, obrigado/a, já não consigo mais.
variants.translation: No, thank you, I can't manage any more.

vocabulary.note: Madeiran regionalism, made with aguardente de cana (sugar cane rum), honey, and lemon juice.
Often a small, traditional establishment.
Common in Portugal for a small black coffee.
Contraction of 'para já', common in spoken Portuguese.
Used in the context of 'Deixa, pago eu' meaning 'Let me, I'll pay'.

dialogues.title: A Round at the Taberna
dialogues.context: At a small, busy taberna in Funchal, two friends, Ana and Rui, are having poncha. The barman is nearby.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Então, Rui, esta poncha tá ótima, não achas?
lines.translation: So, Rui, this poncha is great, don't you think?

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Pois é, Ana! Das melhores que já bebi. Queres outra?
lines.translation: It is, Ana! One of the best I've ever had. Do you want another?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Hum... se calhar sim! Barman! Mais uma rodada de poncha, se faz favor!
lines.translation: Hmm... maybe! Barman! Another round of poncha, please!

lines.speaker: Barman
lines.text: P'ra já! Duas ponchas, certo?
lines.translation: Right away! Two ponchas, right?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Isso mesmo!
lines.translation: That's right!

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Deixa, Ana, pago eu esta.
lines.translation: Leave it, Ana, I'll pay for this one.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Ó Rui, não é preciso! Tu pagaste a última.
lines.translation: Oh Rui, there's no need! You paid for the last one.

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Não faz mal, é um prazer. À nossa, então!
lines.translation: No problem, it's a pleasure. To us, then!

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: À nossa! Saúde!
lines.translation: To us! Cheers!

cultural_notes.title: The Social Code of Paying for Rounds
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, especially in local tabernas, it's common for friends to take turns paying for rounds of drinks. The phrase 'Deixa, pago eu' (Leave it, I'll pay) is a polite and common way to offer to pay for the current round. It's often followed by a friendly back-and-forth, but usually one person will insist. It's a gesture of camaraderie and generosity. Don't be surprised if your Madeiran friends insist on paying for you, and be prepared to reciprocate.

cultural_notes.title: Toasting Etiquette
cultural_notes.body: Toasting is a simple but important social ritual. 'À nossa!' (To us!) is a very common and informal toast among friends. You might also hear 'Saúde!' (Cheers/Health!). It's customary to make eye contact with everyone you're toasting with. If you're offered a drink and don't want it, a polite refusal like 'Não, obrigado/a, estou bem' (No, thank you, I'm good) is perfectly acceptable.

roleplay.scenario: You're at a local taberna in Madeira with a new acquaintance, Miguel. He's just finished his poncha and looks at you.

nodes.npc_text: Então, gostaste da poncha? Queres outra?
nodes.npc_translation: So, did you like the poncha? Do you want another?

options.text: Sim, adorei! E sim, quero outra, se faz favor.
options.translation: Yes, I loved it! And yes, I'd like another, please.
options.feedback: Great! You accepted the offer for another drink.

options.text: Sim, gostei muito, mas não, obrigado, estou bem.
options.translation: Yes, I liked it a lot, but no, thank you, I'm good.
options.feedback: Good job politely refusing the drink.

nodes.npc_text: Ótimo! Barman, mais duas ponchas, se faz favor! Deixa, pago eu.
nodes.npc_translation: Great! Barman, two more ponchas, please! Leave it, I'll pay.

options.text: Não, Miguel, não é preciso! Pago eu esta rodada.
options.translation: No, Miguel, there's no need! I'll pay for this round.
options.feedback: Good, you offered to pay for the round.

options.text: Muito obrigado, Miguel! Na próxima pago eu.
options.translation: Thank you very much, Miguel! Next time I'll pay.
options.feedback: You accepted his offer graciously and promised to pay next time.

nodes.npc_text: Não, não, já paguei a última. É a minha vez. À nossa!
nodes.npc_translation: No, no, I paid for the last one. It's my turn. To us!

options.text: Ah, pois é! Então, à nossa! Saúde!
options.translation: Ah, that's right! So, to us! Cheers!
options.feedback: You accepted his insistence and joined the toast.

options.text: Mesmo assim, queria pagar. Mas ok, à nossa!
options.translation: Even so, I wanted to pay. But okay, to us!
options.feedback: You still expressed a desire to pay but accepted his offer and joined the toast.

nodes.npc_text: Saúde! E então, o que é que achas da ilha até agora?
nodes.npc_translation: Cheers! So, what do you think of the island so far?

nodes.npc_text: Tudo bem, como quiseres. Eu vou pedir mais uma bica. Queres uma?
nodes.npc_translation: Alright, as you wish. I'm going to order another espresso. Do you want one?

options.text: Sim, por favor. Uma bica para mim também.
options.translation: Yes, please. An espresso for me too.
options.feedback: You accepted the offer for a coffee instead.

options.text: Não, obrigado. Mas obrigado pelo convite.
options.translation: No, thank you. But thanks for the invitation.
options.feedback: You politely refused all further drinks.

nodes.npc_text: Barman, mais duas bicas, se faz favor! E então, o que é que vais fazer amanhã?
nodes.npc_translation: Barman, two more espressos, please! So, what are you doing tomorrow?

nodes.npc_text: De nada. Então, o que é que vais fazer amanhã?
nodes.npc_translation: You're welcome. So, what are you doing tomorrow?

situations.title: Madeiran Words in the Wild
situations.summary: Recognising and using genuine Madeiran vocabulary in context — semilha, bica, levada, arraial, "andar de baixo" — knowing when a word is island-only and what mainlanders say instead.

slots.name: local
slots.description: type of local event or place

variants.note: Polite form, using 'o senhor' or 'a senhora' implicitly

vocabulary.note: Madeiran regionalism; 'batata' on mainland Portugal
Common in Portugal for an espresso; 'café' is also used
Madeiran regionalism, referring to the unique irrigation channels and popular walking paths alongside them
Common in Portugal, but particularly significant in Madeiran culture for village feasts
Madeiran regionalism; 'rés-do-chão' on mainland Portugal
Madeiran regionalism; made with aguardente de cana, honey, and lemon/orange juice
Madeiran regionalism; flat bread baked on a basalt stone ('caco')

dialogues.title: Arraial plans and local food
dialogues.context: Two neighbours, Dona Alice and Senhor Jorge, meet on the street and chat about the upcoming local festival and what to eat.

lines.speaker: Dona Alice
lines.text: Olá, Senhor Jorge! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Hello, Mr. Jorge! All good?

lines.speaker: Senhor Jorge
lines.text: Olá, Dona Alice! Tá tudo bem, sim. E consigo? Vai ao arraial este fim de semana?
lines.translation: Hello, Mrs. Alice! Yes, all good. And with you? Are you going to the festival this weekend?

lines.speaker: Dona Alice
lines.text: Pois é, acho que sim! Queria comprar umas semilhas para assar. As do arraial são sempre boas.
lines.translation: Yes, I think so! I wanted to buy some potatoes to roast. The ones at the festival are always good.

lines.speaker: Senhor Jorge
lines.text: Ah, sim! E não se esqueça de um bolo do caco com manteiga de alho! É uma delícia!
lines.translation: Oh, yes! And don't forget a bolo do caco with garlic butter! It's delicious!

lines.speaker: Dona Alice
lines.text: Claro! E depois, uma bica para acordar no dia seguinte, depois de tanta festa!
lines.translation: Of course! And then, an espresso to wake up the next day, after so much partying!

dialogues.title: Asking for directions at the market
dialogues.context: A local resident, Carlos, is at a new grocery store in the market and asks a shop assistant for help finding items and directions to a levada.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Com licença, sabe onde é o andar de baixo? Não encontro as frutas.
lines.translation: Excuse me, do you know where the ground floor is? I can't find the fruit.

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: Bom dia! O andar de baixo é lá no fundo, à esquerda, depois das escadas. Lá tem as frutas e as semilhas frescas.
lines.translation: Good morning! The ground floor is at the back, to the left, after the stairs. There you'll find fresh fruit and potatoes.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Ah, muito obrigado! E já agora, sabe se a levada dos Piornais está aberta para passeios?
lines.translation: Ah, thank you very much! And by the way, do you know if the Piornais levada is open for walks?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: A dos Piornais? Acho que sim, esteve fechada um bocado para obras, mas agora tá aberta. É um passeio muito bonito!
lines.translation: The Piornais one? I think so, it was closed for a bit for works, but now it's open. It's a very beautiful walk!

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Ótimo! Depois de umas compras, vou lá dar um salto. E uma bica, claro!
lines.translation: Great! After some shopping, I'll pop over there. And an espresso, of course!

cultural_notes.title: Madeiran vs. Mainland Portuguese Vocabulary
cultural_notes.body: Madeira has several unique words and expressions. For example, 'semilha' is used for 'potato' instead of 'batata', and 'andar de baixo' refers to the 'ground floor' instead of 'rés-do-chão'. While mainlanders would understand these, using them shows you're familiar with local customs. 'Levada' is a term unique to Madeira, referring to the island's distinctive irrigation channels, many of which are now popular walking trails. 'Arraial' refers to a local festival, often a village feast with food, music, and religious celebrations, a very important part of Madeiran culture.

cultural_notes.title: Ordering Coffee: 'Bica' and 'Café'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, including Madeira, the most common way to order an espresso is simply 'um café'. However, 'uma bica' is also widely understood and used, especially in Lisbon and the south of Portugal, and by extension, in Madeira. Both terms refer to the same small, strong coffee. Using 'bica' in Madeira is perfectly natural and shows familiarity.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a small local grocery store in Madeira, asking the owner about some specific Madeiran products and local information.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Posso ajudar em alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! Can I help you with anything?

options.text: Sim, queria umas semilhas, por favor.
options.translation: Yes, I'd like some potatoes, please.
options.feedback: Great start! You asked for 'semilhas', the Madeiran word for potatoes.

options.text: Estou à procura de bolo do caco fresco.
options.translation: I'm looking for fresh bolo do caco.
options.feedback: Excellent! You asked for 'bolo do caco', a famous Madeiran bread.

nodes.npc_text: Claro! Quantas semilhas quer? Temos umas ótimas hoje.
nodes.npc_translation: Of course! How many potatoes do you want? We have some great ones today.

options.text: Um quilo, por favor. E sabe onde posso encontrar poncha?
options.translation: One kilo, please. And do you know where I can find poncha?
options.feedback: Good, you specified the quantity and then asked about 'poncha', another Madeiran specialty.

options.text: Dois quilos. E onde é que costumam fazer os arraiais maiores?
options.translation: Two kilos. And where do they usually hold the bigger festivals?
options.feedback: Well done! You specified the quantity and then asked about 'arraiais', the local festivals.

nodes.npc_text: Bolo do caco? Acabou de chegar! Está no andar de baixo, ao lado dos queijos.
nodes.npc_translation: Bolo do caco? It just arrived! It's on the ground floor, next to the cheeses.

options.text: Ah, o andar de baixo! Obrigada. E alguma levada bonita por perto?
options.translation: Ah, the ground floor! Thank you. And any nice levada nearby?
options.feedback: Perfect! You understood 'andar de baixo' and then asked about a 'levada'.

options.text: Ótimo! E depois de ir ao andar de baixo, onde posso beber uma boa bica?
options.translation: Great! And after going to the ground floor, where can I drink a good espresso?
options.feedback: Good, you used 'andar de baixo' correctly and then asked for a 'bica'.

nodes.npc_text: Poncha? Temos ali no balcão, já feita. Se quiser a mais tradicional, vá ao bar do senhor João, ali na rua de baixo.
nodes.npc_translation: Poncha? We have it here at the counter, already made. If you want the more traditional one, go to Mr. João's bar, down that street.

nodes.npc_text: Os arraiais maiores são mais nos concelhos, como o de Santana ou o Monte. Mas aqui na freguesia também há sempre um arraial, especialmente no verão!
nodes.npc_translation: The bigger festivals are more in the municipalities, like Santana or Monte. But here in the parish, there's always a festival too, especially in summer!

nodes.npc_text: Sim, a levada do Norte passa aqui perto. É um passeio fácil e com vistas lindas. É só seguir aquela rua à direita.
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, the North levada passes nearby. It's an easy walk with beautiful views. Just follow that street to the right.

nodes.npc_text: Uma bica? No café da Dona Rosa, mesmo em frente. A bica dela é a melhor da zona!
nodes.npc_translation: An espresso? At Dona Rosa's café, right across the street. Her espresso is the best in the area!

mission.title: Find Madeiran Specialties and Local Spots

likely_responses: É lá no fundo, à direita.

goals: Recognise and use Madeiran-specific vocabulary (semilha, andar de baixo, levada, arraial, poncha, bolo do caco) in context.
Understand when a word is Madeiran vs. mainland Portuguese.
Ask for local products and information in a market or local shop setting.
Engage in short, natural conversations about local culture and recommendations.
